skill.name: ai-rag-pipeline
skill.description: Build retrieval augmented generation workflows that combine web search, content extraction, and LLM analysis through the inference.sh CLI.
skill.summary: Create grounded RAG research workflows with search, extraction, LLM synthesis, and source attribution.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command and network patterns. Review confirms the remote curl-to-shell install command as a high-risk supply-chain pattern, while Markdown backtick and weak-cryptography alerts are false positives. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so the skill is not blocked but should not publish without install and quoting changes.

high_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to run curl against https://cli.inference.sh and pipe the result directly to sh before login. This is a true positive because any compromise of the remote script, hosting path, or transport would execute code on the user machine before independent verification. The install note describes checksum verification, but the repository does not provide the script contents for audit.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl pipe-to-shell command is present in the quick start, and the surrounding text confirms it is an installation path. I did not find evidence of malicious intent, but the execution pattern is a well-known supply-chain risk.

medium_findings.title: Shell Examples Interpolate Unsanitized Values
medium_findings.description: Several copy-paste examples place shell variables inside JSON strings and prompts. This is legitimate tutorial content, but user-controlled values such as claims, topics, or query arguments can break quoting, alter requests, or inject prompt content when reused in automation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly use shell variables inside command inputs. The risk depends on how users adapt the snippets, so confidence is high for the unsafe pattern but not for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: External Network Services Handle Research Data
low_findings.description: The skill is designed around inference.sh, Tavily, Exa, and OpenRouter workflows. This is expected for RAG, but users may send queries, URLs, extracted text, and research topics to third-party services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly documents external hosted services and remote app IDs. The data exposure risk is inherent to the stated capability, not hidden exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Most Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown code fences, inline app identifiers, diagrams, or documentation formatting. I did not find Ruby backtick execution semantics in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visibly Markdown tables, diagrams, and command examples. They are not Ruby code and do not execute by being present in the skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts point to prose and documentation links, while the install note mentions SHA-256 checksum verification. No weak cryptographic implementation was found in the audited file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines do not implement cryptography, and SHA-256 is referenced as a checksum. This supports dismissal of the high blocker classification for those hits.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start instructs users to run curl against https://cli.inference.sh and pipe the result directly to sh before login. This is a true positive because any compromise of the remote script, hosting path, or transport would execute code on the user machine before independent verification. The install note describes checksum verification, but the repository does not provide the script contents for audit.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact curl pipe-to-shell command is present in the quick start, and the surrounding text confirms it is an installation path. I did not find evidence of malicious intent, but the execution pattern is a well-known supply-chain risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Examples Interpolate Unsanitized Values
dangerous_patterns.description: Several copy-paste examples place shell variables inside JSON strings and prompts. This is legitimate tutorial content, but user-controlled values such as claims, topics, or query arguments can break quoting, alter requests, or inject prompt content when reused in automation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly use shell variables inside command inputs. The risk depends on how users adapt the snippets, so confidence is high for the unsafe pattern but not for malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Ungrounded AI answers can miss sources and current context. This skill shows how to combine web retrieval, extraction, and LLM analysis with citations.
actual_capabilities: Explains core RAG patterns for search, extraction, augmentation, and LLM generation.
Shows inference.sh CLI workflows using Tavily Search, Exa Search, Exa Answer, and OpenRouter models.
Provides examples for fact-checking, multi-source research, report generation, and quick answers with sources.
Demonstrates context management by summarizing long search results before deeper analysis.
Lists related skills and documentation links for extending research agents.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and access to external hosted services.
Does not include a local vector database, embedding store, or production deployment code.
Example shell snippets need safer quoting before use with untrusted input.
Source quality depends on the selected search provider and retrieved web pages.

use_cases.title: Research Briefs With Sources
use_cases.description: Create concise research summaries that combine current web results with cited LLM synthesis.

use_cases.title: Claim Verification Workflows
use_cases.description: Check a claim against search evidence and ask the model for support, contradictions, and uncertainty.

use_cases.title: Agent Tool Planning
use_cases.description: Design an agent workflow that calls search, extraction, and model tools in a repeatable sequence.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Basic RAG Search
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a simple RAG workflow for my topic: [topic]. Include the search step, model step, and expected cited output.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Sources
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a multi-source research workflow for [topic]. Compare Tavily and Exa results, then ask Claude to identify themes and contradictions.

prompt_templates.title: Fact-Check A Claim
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the fact-checking pattern to evaluate this claim: [claim]. Show what evidence to retrieve and what verdict structure to request.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Reusable Research Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reusable RAG research agent for [domain]. Include retrieval tools, extraction rules, context limits, citation requirements, and failure handling.

output_examples.input: Research the latest adoption trends for enterprise AI assistants.
output_examples.output: A sourced brief with an executive summary, key adoption drivers, market signals, risks, and links to the retrieved sources.

output_examples.input: Fact-check whether a technology claim is supported by current evidence.
output_examples.output: A verdict with supporting evidence, contradicting evidence, confidence level, and cited references.

output_examples.input: Summarize two extracted industry reports into strategic recommendations.
output_examples.output: A structured report with key findings, data points, recommendations, and source attribution.

best_practices: Use specific queries with time ranges, entities, and expected evidence types.
Summarize long retrieval results before sending them into larger analysis prompts.
Require source citations and separate retrieved facts from model interpretation.
anti_patterns: Do not paste sensitive internal data into external search or model services without approval.
Do not use copied shell snippets with untrusted input until quoting is hardened.
Do not treat model answers as verified facts without checking the cited sources.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you design RAG workflows that retrieve web information, add it to prompts, and generate sourced answers.

faq.question: Which tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references inference.sh CLI apps for Tavily, Exa, and OpenRouter models such as Claude, GPT-4o, and Gemini.

faq.question: Does it include production application code?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow patterns and CLI examples, not a complete deployed RAG service.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What security review result should users know?
faq.answer: The audit flags the curl-to-shell installation command and shell quoting examples as risks that need hardening.

faq.question: How should I handle private information?
faq.answer: Avoid sending private documents, secrets, or regulated data to external search and model providers without governance approval.
